topic: Area of Study 1: Western Classical Tradition 1650-1910
subject: music

items.q: What distinguishes Classical (1750-1820) from Baroque style?
items.correct: Clearer melodic phrases, homophonic texture, balanced structures like sonata form.
items.random_wrong: Classical means any old serious music.
items.why: Students use 'classical' loosely. The Classical period specifically reacted against Baroque complexity - simpler textures, clearer cadences, and balanced phrase structures (Mozart, Haydn, early Beethoven).

items.q: What does the time signature 6/8 indicate?
items.correct: Two beats per bar, each beat divided into three (compound duple).
items.misconception: Six beats per bar, all equal in stress.
items.why: Students count 6/8 as six equal beats. In practice it's felt as TWO dotted-crotchet beats subdivided into three quavers each - compound duple, not simple sextuple.

items.q: What is a balanced antecedent-consequent phrase?
items.correct: Two phrases of equal length where the first sounds open (question) and the second resolves (answer).
items.misconception: Any two phrases of similar style.
items.random_wrong: A long melody with no breaks.
items.why: Students miss the FUNCTIONAL relationship. The antecedent typically ends on an imperfect cadence (open); the consequent answers with a perfect cadence (closed). Length matches; cadences contrast.

items.q: What is the name of the broken-chord accompaniment pattern common in Mozart's piano sonatas?
items.why: It's Alberti bass. “Walking bass” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Which composer is most associated with the late-eighteenth-century Sturm und Drang style?
items.why: It's Haydn. “Mozart” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Approximately in what year does the classical era conventionally begin?
items.why: Exact answer: 1750. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
